feedback: The model demonstrates solid malware analysis capability with an exact match accuracy of 52.9% (9/17 questions) and a strong average Jaccard similarity of 0.838. Zero parse failures indicate reliable output formatting. The model performs best on questions requiring precise identification of Defense Evasion techniques, correctly matching gold answers on the majority of questions. However, it exhibits a consistent pattern of over-selection, adding extra options beyond the gold set in 7 out of 8 incorrect answers, with only 1 case of pure under-selection. The average predicted answer count (4.06) exceeds the average gold answer count (3.94) slightly, but the over-selection pattern is more nuanced: the model tends to include plausible-but-incorrect options rather than missing correct ones. Key weaknesses include: (1) Ransomware family questions show the worst performance (1/5 exact matches, 0.733 avg Jaccard), particularly in distinguishing which behaviors constitute Defense Evasion vs. Impact/Execution. The model incorrectly includes shadow copy deletion via WMIC (Impact/T1490), file renaming with double extensions (Impact), and writing PE headers to disk (Ingress Tool Transfer) as Defense Evasion. (2) Killers/EDR family shows mixed performance (2/4 exact matches), with the model failing to recognize file deletion (T1070.004) as Defense Evasion in one case, and over-including LoadLibrary and CreateThread calls as evasion in another. (3) Remcos family shows poor performance (1/3 exact matches), with the model incorrectly classifying writing PE file headers and targeting legacy systems as Defense Evasion techniques, and missing the registry Run key persistence technique that also constitutes Defense Evasion (T1547.001 mapped under Defense Evasion in the report). (4) The model struggles with boundary cases: behaviors like AdjustTokenPrivileges and proxy settings modification are sometimes correctly included, sometimes not, suggesting inconsistent ATT&CK mapping knowledge. Training should focus on: (a) distinguishing Impact vs. Defense Evasion for ransomware-specific behaviors (shadow copy deletion, file renaming); (b) correctly identifying when persistence mechanisms (Run keys) are also mapped as Defense Evasion in sandbox reports; (c) avoiding over-inclusion of Ingress Tool Transfer and Discovery behaviors as Defense Evasion; (d) medium-difficulty questions across all attack families to improve calibration.
strengths: Perfect parse rate: all 17 model answers were correctly formatted and parseable
Strong performance on infostealers (3/4 exact matches, 0.917 avg Jaccard)
Strong performance on um_unhooking (3/4 exact matches, 0.917 avg Jaccard)
High macro recall (0.911) indicating the model rarely misses correct options
Correctly identifies obfuscation/packing, PAGE_GUARD memory allocation, APC injection, and registry tracing modifications as Defense Evasion across multiple question types
Consistent ability to distinguish Discovery behaviors (CreateToolhelp32Snapshot, registry reads for computer name/GUID) from Defense Evasion

weaknesses.description: Model incorrectly includes ransomware-specific Impact behaviors (shadow copy deletion via WMIC, file renaming with double extensions) and Ingress Tool Transfer behaviors (writing PE headers, dropping executables) as Defense Evasion. Also incorrectly includes AdjustTokenPrivileges in Spora question where gold excludes it.

In 7 of 8 incorrect answers, the model adds extra options beyond the gold set. Common over-inclusions: writing PE file headers to disk (T1105, not Defense Evasion), AdjustTokenPrivileges when not in gold, LoadLibrary/CreateThread as evasion, targeting 32-bit compatibility as evasion, and ML detection threshold manipulation.

Model misclassifies writing PE file headers and 32-bit PE targeting as Defense Evasion (question 10), and fails to include registry Run key (T1547.001) as Defense Evasion in question 9 where it was mapped under Defense Evasion in the report.

Model missed file deletion/marking for deletion (T1070.004) as Defense Evasion in question 3, and failed to include AdjustTokenPrivileges (T1134) as Defense Evasion in question 4. Also over-included LoadLibrary and CreateThread in question 5.

Model struggles to distinguish when behaviors like shadow copy deletion (T1490, Impact), ingress tool transfer (T1105), and service execution are NOT Defense Evasion, even when they appear in sandbox reports alongside Defense Evasion techniques.

In question 1 (infostealers), model missed registry Run key modification (T1547.001) which was mapped as Defense Evasion in the report. Model may not recognize that some persistence techniques are dual-tagged as Defense Evasion.
All questions are hard difficulty, making difficulty-specific analysis limited, but the 47% miss rate on hard questions suggests room for improvement on nuanced ATT&CK mappings.

question.question: A sandbox detonation of a ransomware sample (vx_family: Ransom.Diavolo.Generic) shows the following process tree: hash.exe spawns cmd.exe, which executes WMIC.exe with 'shadowcopy where ID=... delete'. Which of the following behaviors observed in this report should be classified as Defense Evasion techniques according to the MITRE ATT&CK framework?

B. Inhibit System Recovery (T1490) — WMIC.exe deletes volume shadow copies

D. Masquerade File Type (T1036.008) — executable dropped with .txt extension
E. Data Encrypted for Impact (T1486) — files are encrypted by the ransomware
